--- title: "General Social Survey Analysis" format: html description: "An Analysis of the General Social Survey (GSS) Data: the GSS is one of the most important monitors of American societial attitudes and behaviors over the last 50 years. it measures behaviours since 1972-current. This analysis only filtered for (1972-2014) period" author: "Adeyeye Seyi Joseph" date: "04/06/2026" image: "air.jpg" categories: - People - Surveys - Society - USA - CategoricalData - Python --- ::: {#5e424251 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} # firstly we import the needed modules/libraries: import pandas as pd import numpy as np from pandas import read_csv import seaborn as sns import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import datetime as dt import plotly.express as px from statsmodels.graphics.mosaicplot import mosaic import warnings warnings.filterwarnings("ignore") ``` ::: ::: {#3c90d0f2 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Read in the dataset: df = pd.read_csv("C:\\Users\\hp\\Desktop\\python\\projects\\gss_sub.csv") df ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: {#03d75369 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Taking an overview of the dataset: df.info() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` RangeIndex: 59599 entries, 0 to 59598 Data columns (total 16 columns): # Column Non-Null Count Dtype --- ------ -------------- ----- 0 year 59599 non-null float64 1 id 59599 non-null float64 2 labor_status 59583 non-null object 3 self_employed 59306 non-null object 4 marital_status 59575 non-null object 5 n_siblings 56682 non-null float64 6 age 59599 non-null float64 7 high_school 59440 non-null float64 8 degree 59464 non-null object 9 political_affiliation 59257 non-null object 10 environment 59388 non-null object 11 law_enforcement 59378 non-null object 12 drugs 59380 non-null object 13 space_exploration 59596 non-null object 14 inequality 1532 non-null float64 15 household_size 59599 non-null float64 dtypes: float64(7), object(9) memory usage: 7.3+ MB ``` ::: ::: ::: {#358c0a6f .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} # inspecting individual cat cols : df.describe(include='object') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}

create a dict of columns and data type mapping dict_conversion = {k: "category" for k in df.select_dtypes(include="object").columns} dict_conversion # convert using the astype method: df = df.astype(dict_conversion) df.info() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` RangeIndex: 59599 entries, 0 to 59598 Data columns (total 16 columns): # Column Non-Null Count Dtype --- ------ -------------- ----- 0 year 59599 non-null float64 1 id 59599 non-null float64 2 labor_status 59583 non-null category 3 self_employed 59306 non-null category 4 marital_status 59575 non-null category 5 n_siblings 56682 non-null float64 6 age 59599 non-null float64 7 high_school 59440 non-null float64 8 degree 59464 non-null category 9 political_affiliation 59257 non-null category 10 environment 59388 non-null category 11 law_enforcement 59378 non-null category 12 drugs 59380 non-null category 13 space_exploration 59596 non-null category 14 inequality 1532 non-null float64 15 household_size 59599 non-null float64 dtypes: category(9), float64(7) memory usage: 3.7 MB ``` ::: ::: ::: {#6f51aa61 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} # inspecting a single cat col levels: df["labor_status"].cat.categories ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ``` Index(['KEEPING HOUSE', 'OTHER', 'RETIRED', 'SCHOOL', 'TEMP NOT WORKING', 'UNEMPL, LAID OFF', 'WORKING FULLTIME', 'WORKING PARTTIME'], dtype='object') ``` ::: ::: ::: {#0e31e989 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} # create a dict to collapse: new_labour_status = {'UNEMPL, LAID OFF' : 'UNEMPLOYED', 'TEMP NOT WORKING': 'UNEMPLOYED', 'WORKING FULLTIME' : 'EMPLOYED', 'WORKING PARTTIME' : 'EMPLOYED' } # Replace the values in the column and reset as a category: df["labour_status_clean"] = ( df["labor_status"] .astype(str) .replace(new_labour_status) .astype("category") ) # preview new col : df["labour_status_clean"].value_counts(normalize=True) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ``` labour_status_clean EMPLOYED 0.596520 KEEPING HOUSE 0.161915 RETIRED 0.135942 UNEMPLOYED 0.054196 SCHOOL 0.030890 OTHER 0.020269 nan 0.000268 Name: proportion, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#1573c4e9 .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} # inspecting vars or cols with same cat levels/encoding: print(df.environment.cat.categories) print(df.drugs.cat.categories) print(df.law_enforcement.cat.categories) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Index(['ABOUT RIGHT', 'DK', 'IAP', 'TOO LITTLE', 'TOO MUCH'], dtype='object') Index(['ABOUT RIGHT', 'DK', 'IAP', 'TOO LITTLE', 'TOO MUCH'], dtype='object') Index(['ABOUT RIGHT', 'DK', 'IAP', 'TOO LITTLE', 'TOO MUCH'], dtype='object') ``` ::: ::: ::: {#98ac55c6 .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Re-ordering cat vars : # Set the new order: new_order = ['TOO LITTLE', 'TOO MUCH', 'ABOUT RIGHT','DK', 'IAP'] cat_to_remove = ['DK', 'IAP'] # loop through each col : for col in ["environment","law_enforcement", "drugs"]: # reorder and remove the categories df[col + "_clean"] = df[col].cat.reorder_categories(new_order, ordered=True) df[col + "_clean"] = df[col + "_clean"].cat.remove_categories(cat_to_remove) # preview of the col's categories df["environment_clean"].cat.categories ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=10} ``` Index(['TOO LITTLE', 'TOO MUCH', 'ABOUT RIGHT'], dtype='object') ``` ::: ::: ::: {#257fadbd .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} df["degree"].cat.categories.tolist() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ``` ['BACHELOR', 'DK', 'GRADUATE', 'HIGH SCHOOL', 'JUNIOR COLLEGE', 'LT HIGH SCHOOL'] ``` ::: ::: ::: {#235a874f .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} # defining a dict to map old degree levels to new ones: new_degree = {'LT HIGH SCHOOL' : 'HIGH SCHOOL', 'BACHELOR' : 'COLLEGE/UNIVERSITY', 'GRADUATE' : 'COLLEGE/UNIVERSITY', 'JUNIOR COLLEGE': 'COLLEGE/UNIVERSITY' } # replace old degree categories with new ones and convert to categorical: df["degree_clean"] = df["degree"].astype(str).replace(new_degree).astype("category") # Remove the 'DK' level from the new degree col: df["degree_clean"] = ( df["degree_clean"] .cat.remove_categories(['DK']) ) # Re-ordered the degree_clean levels and set as ordered: df["degree_clean"] = pd.Categorical( df["degree_clean"], categories=['HIGH SCHOOL', 'COLLEGE/UNIVERSITY'], ordered=True ) # preview the new col df["degree_clean"].value_counts(normalize=True) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ``` degree_clean HIGH SCHOOL 0.732796 COLLEGE/UNIVERSITY 0.267204 Name: proportion, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#bca60393 .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} # binning date var/col along decade lines: df["year"] = df["year"].astype("Int64") decade_boundaries = [(1970, 1979), (1979, 1989),(1989, 1999), (1999, 2009), (2009, 2019)] decade_labels = ["1970s", "1980s", "1990s", "2000s", "2010s"] # set the bins and cut the Dataframe: bins = pd.IntervalIndex.from_tuples(decade_boundaries) df["decade"] = pd.cut(df["year"], bins) # rename the newly created decade col df["decade"] = df["decade"].cat.rename_categories(decade_labels) # preview new col df[["year", "decade"]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=13} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: {#209df529 .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} # visualizing categorical var: fig = px.bar(df["labour_status_clean"].value_counts(), template = "plotly_white", labels={"labour_status_clean": "Labour Status", "value":"Count of Respondents"}) # update the layout fig.update_layout(showlegend=False) fig.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {#9fa6e765 .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} # visualizing categorical vars: making adjustments to our first plot fig = px.bar(df["labour_status_clean"].value_counts(ascending=True), template = "plotly_white", orientation ="h", title="Labour Status by Respondents", labels={"labour_status_clean": "Labour Status", "value":"Count of Respondents"}) # update the layout fig.update_layout(showlegend=False) fig.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {#ad208751 .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} # showing categorical averages : household_by_decade = df.groupby(["decade", "degree_clean"], as_index=False)["household_size"].mean().round(2) household_by_decade ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=16} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: {#68a342dc .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} # visualizing using a boxplot: fig = px.box(df, x="age", y="labour_status_clean", title="Age Distribution by Labour Status", labels={"age": "Age", "labour_status_clean":"Labour Status"}) fig.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {#be5139fe .cell execution_count=19} ``` {.python .cell-code} # we can use a frequency table to show this relatiobship to get a overview of such relatiobship #between the two vars pd.crosstab(df["degree_clean"], df["law_enforcement_clean"]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=19} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: {#4e2b7729 .cell execution_count=20} ``` {.python .cell-code} # using a mosaic plot to show result: # mosaic plot enables one to show the relationship between two categorical variables: it helps #to #give a sense of proportions between cat vars mosaic(df, ["degree_clean", "law_enforcement_clean"], title="Law enforcement opinions/stance by Degree") plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](Analyzing_cat_data_files/figure-html/cell-21-output-1.png){width=642 height=431} ::: ::: ::: {#9b17c538 .cell execution_count=21} ``` {.python .cell-code} # visualizing marital rates using line chart: marital_rates = df.groupby(["year"], as_index=False)["marital_status"].value_counts(normalize=True) marital_rates ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=21} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ### Summary of findings: 1. Employed persons formed the largest chunk of our respondents with over 35,000 thousand in the General Social Survey Data. 2. The average household size of the High School leaver has consistently been bigger across years(decades) than the household size of a university graduate: the average household size of the High school leaver in the 1970s, was 3.27, while the household size of university graduate in the 1970s was 3.03. In 2010s, the average household size of High school leavers was 2.47, while that of university graduates stood at 2.3. 3. Many people with High School certs are not okay with spending /budgetary allocations for law enforcement , while those with college degrees feels the spending on law enforcement is too much. 4. Marital rates ( number of those who got marrried) have been declining over the decades; over 72% were married in 1972, but as at 2010, the figure stands at 44%. It leads credence to the observations that the institution of marriage is experiencing various challenges and problems affectings its stability, vibrancy and net benefits(good) to the general society. 5. Education wise : The number of of people(respondents) who have High school certs have been declining with its peak at 88% in 1970s, the number of people with university degree in 1970 was 12%. while the number of people with university degree continues to gain increase across years while the proportion of persons with High school certs has steadily plummetted. ### Recommendations: 1. Governments of various nations should pay urgent attention to the decline in marital rates over years: for instance, South Korea/Japan have one of the world's lowest birth rates. These stats are not good for industried economies which seek to perpetuate their positive contribution to the civilization of the human race. Governments/local authorities should incentivize young people to get married , give birth and contribute meaningfully to the good of the country. 2. Educated persons should be encourage to give birth to more children owning to the fact that, the average university graduates is more financial capable of rearing more children than persons with only High school certs. ### Original Datasets: !(The original datasets can be accessed on Kaggle at:)[https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/norc/general-social-survey?select=gss.csv]
